summary: Nadia reflects on her decision to leave Collaborative Fund, framing it through Milan Kundera's concept of 'Es muss sein' — the idea that our most meaningful choices feel less like free will and more like inescapable fate. She joined to work on an experimental growth fund but was redirected to deploying a seed stage fund. Despite loving the organization and its mission, she spent a year trying to suppress the pull toward something else. She announces her departure with gratitude and signals her next focus: finding new ways to direct capital toward valuable internet-powered ideas.
key_points: Our most meaningful life choices feel not like free will but like inescapable imperatives — fate rather than decision
Even when external circumstances seem ideal ('paper lives check all the boxes'), internal callings cannot be permanently suppressed
She valued Collaborative Fund's mission deeply but recognized that the specific role diverged from where she felt most compelled to work
Her next direction — directing capital toward valuable ideas powering the internet — foreshadows her later work on open source sustainability
money_quotes: We do not make them out of free will; our choices are an inescapable fate, an "imperative enslaving us".
For the past year I have tried to avoid my fate. But we can't ignore the things that scream most loudly in our heads, even when our paper lives seem to check all the boxes.
Venture capital showed me the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition.
It also gave me deep faith in the capacity of my fellow humans for kindness and generosity.
Finding new ways to direct capital towards valuable ideas that power the internet.
key_insight: The most important life transitions come not from rational deliberation but from an inner imperative that becomes impossible to ignore, even when everything on paper looks right.
